papers.title: The fastest stars in the Galaxy
papers.author_names: KAREEM EL-BADRY KEN J. SHEN VEDANT CHANDRA EVAN B. BAUER JIM FULLER JAY STRADER LAURA CHOMIUK ROHAN P. NAIDU ILARIA CAIAZZO ANTONIO C. RODRIGUEZ PRANAV NAGARAJAN NATSUKO YAMAGUCHI ZACHARY P. VANDERBOSCH BENJAMIN R. ROULSTON JAN VAN ROESTEL BORIS GANSICKE JIWON JESSE HAN KEVIN B. BURDGE ALEXEI V. FILIPPENKO THOMAS G. BRINK WEIKANG ZHENG

abstract.text: We report a spectroscopic search for hypervelocity white dwarfs (WDs) that are runaways from Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) and related thermonuclear explosions. Candidates are selected from Gaia data with high tangential velocities and blue colors. We find six new runaways, including four stars with radial velocities (RVs) $>1000\\\\,\\\\rm km\\\\,s^{-1}$ and total space velocities $\\\\gtrsim 1300\\\\,\\\\rm km\\\\,s^{-1}$. These are most likely the surviving donors from double-degenerate binaries in which the other WD exploded. The other two objects have lower minimum velocities, $\\\\gtrsim 600\\\\,\\\\rm km\\\\,s^{-1}$, and may have formed through a different mechanism, such as pure deflagration of a WD in a Type Iax supernova. The four fastest stars are hotter and smaller than the previously known "D$^6$ stars," with effective temperatures ranging from $\\\\sim$20,000 to $\\\\sim$130,000 K and radii of $\\\\sim 0.02-0.10\\\\,R_{\\\\odot}$. Three of these have carbon-dominated atmospheres, and one has a helium-dominated atmosphere. Two stars have RVs of $-1694$ and $-2285\\\\rm \\\\,km\\\\,s^{-1}$ -- the fastest systemic stellar RVs ever measured. Their inferred birth velocities, $\\\\sim 2200-2500\\\\,\\\\rm km\\\\,s^{-1}$, imply that both WDs in the progenitor binary had masses $>1.0\\\\,M_{\\\\odot}$. The high observed velocities suggest that a dominant fraction of the observed hypervelocity WD population comes from double-degenerate binaries whose total mass significantly exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit. However, the two nearest and faintest D$^6$ stars have the lowest velocities and masses, suggesting that observational selection effects favor rarer, higher-mass stars. A significant population of fainter low-mass runaways may still await discovery. We infer a birth rate of D$^6$ stars that is consistent with the SN Ia rate. The birth rate is poorly constrained, however, because the luminosities and lifetimes of $\\\\rm D^6$ stars are uncertain.

triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] A spectroscopic survey identifies six hypervelocity white dwarfs (WDs) with space velocities ≥1300 km/s, four of which exceed 1000 km/s radial velocity—among the fastest stellar motions ever recorded. These stars, including two with RVs of −1694 and −2285 km/s, are interpreted as surviving donors from double-degenerate binary systems where one WD exploded in a double-detonation (D⁶ scenario), implying progenitor WD masses >1.0 M⊙ and total binary masses exceeding the Chandrasekhar limit. The D⁶ stars are hotter (20,000–130,000 K), smaller (0.02–0.10 R⊙), and chemically peculiar (carbon- or helium-dominated), with birth velocities suggesting a dominant origin from double-detonation channels. Observational selection biases favor high-mass, high-velocity stars, implying a likely underrepresentation of fainter, lower-mass runaways, though their birth rate is consistent with the SN Ia rate.

source_fields.title: The fastest stars in the Galaxy

source_fields.sections_text: Footnotes: The footnotes provide contextual details on observational sources, stellar evolution assumptions, and uncertainties regarding the scale height and accretion properties of stars in the study. Discussion: Kinematic modeling shows that three of four new D⁶ stars require low-mass donors (with ejection velocities ~1000–1500 km s⁻¹), while only one requires a high-mass donor, suggesting that WD+WD mergers with low-mass donors are more common and likely do not produce hypervelocity WDs via double detonation. Conclusions: This study identifies six high-confidence hypervelocity white dwarfs, including extremely hot and compact objects with unprecedented velocities, likely originating from thermonuclear runaways in double-white-dwarf binaries, with implications for progenitor binary masses and the long-term fate of such systems in the Milky Way. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel observational strategy to identify runaway white dwarfs from double-degenerate supernovae by detecting hydrogen-deficient, tidally inflated stars with large tangential velocities and specific spectral signatures, thereby solving the long-standing problem of determining the fraction of Type Ia supernovae originating from the double-degenerate channel. Kinematic Modeling: The paper proposes a Bayesian inference method that uses parallax, proper motion, and radial velocity data to reconstruct the 3D trajectories of stars, infers their birth ejection velocities by subtracting Galactic rotation velocities at disk crossing points, and imposes a physical upper limit of 3000 km/s on ejection velocities to constrain binary merger scenarios. Spectroscopic Analysis: Spectroscopic analysis of the hypervelocity white dwarfs reveals unusual, heterogeneous spectra that are best modeled with synthetic spectra assuming carbon-oxygen or helium-dominated atmospheres, enabling RV measurements and initial estimates of effective temperature and surface composition without quantitative abundance determinations. Spectral Energy Distributions: This section presents radius constraints for a sample of stars derived from broadband spectral energy distributions, with distance uncertainties identified as the dominant source of radius uncertainty, and provides corresponding surface gravity estimates assuming a 1 M☉ mass. Search For Hypervelocity Wds With Gaia: This section describes a method to identify hypervelocity white dwarfs using Gaia data by selecting stars with high tangential velocities and well-constrained parallaxes, while minimizing contamination from foreground stars through astrometric and photometric criteria. MASSES OF THE $\\mathrm{D}^6$ PROGENITORS: The masses of the $\\mathrm{D}^6$ progenitor binaries are constrained by comparing observed ejection velocities to theoretical predictions, showing that only systems with high total masses (e.g., $\\gtrsim 2.0M_{\\odot}$) can plausibly explain the ejection velocities of certain candidates, while others—like J0927-6335—exceed the maximum predicted velocities and are thus unlikely to arise from the $\\mathrm{D}^6$ scenario. Census Of The Hypervelocity Wd Population: This section analyzes the kinematic and photometric properties of hypervelocity white dwarf candidates, revealing distinct clumps in the color-magnitude diagram for D⁶ stars and finding that their differing ejection velocities make an evolutionary sequence from cool to hot D⁶ stars unlikely.

abstract: We report a spectroscopic search for hypervelocity white dwarfs (WDs) that are runaways from Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) and related thermonuclear explosions.

The four fastest stars are hotter and smaller than the previously known "D$^6$ stars," with effective temperatures ranging from $\\\\sim$20,000 to $\\\\sim$130,000 K and radii of $\\\\sim 0.02-0.10\\\\
Two stars have RVs of $-1694$ and $-2285\\\\rm \\\\,km\\\\,s^{-1}$ -- the fastest systemic stellar RVs ever measured.
However, the two nearest and faintest D$^6$ stars have the lowest velocities and masses, suggesting that observational selection effects favor rarer, higher-mass stars.
We infer a birth rate of D$^6$ stars that is consistent with the SN Ia rate. The birth rate is poorly constrained, however, because the luminosities and lifetimes of $\\\\rm D^6$ stars are uncertain.
title: The fastest stars in the Galaxy

catalog_assessment.evidence: Reports discovery of six new hypervelocity white dwarfs with measured radial velocities, proper motions, temperatures, radii, and atmospheric compositions from Gaia data and spectroscopic follow-up.

papers.title: On Stellar Migration from Andromeda to the Milky Way
papers.author_names: Lukas Gülzow Malcolm Fairbairn Dominik J. Schwarz

abstract.text: Recent Gaia observations suggest that some hypervelocity stars (HVSs) might originate from outside the Galaxy. We ask if these HVSs could come from as far as Andromeda. Therefore, we simulate HVSs originating in Andromeda with initial conditions based on attributes of high-velocity stars measured in the Milky Way and a simple model for the gravitational potential of Andromeda and the Milky Way. We evaluate the validity of this scenario based on the simulation results. While we expect the vast majority of HVSs in our Galaxy will originate here, we expect the number of stars present from Andromeda at any one time to be between twelve and 3910, depending upon model assumptions. Further, we analyse the properties of HVSs that are able to reach the Milky Way. We discuss whether they could be detected experimentally based on recent constraints set on the ejection rate of HVSs from the Milky Way centre

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: Simulations show that a small fraction of hypervelocity stars ejected from Andromeda can reach the Milky Way, forming an isotropic and homogeneous distribution around the galactic center with velocities pointing away from Andromeda, consistent with observational data and detectable in future studies. Introduction: This paper presents a novel dynamical simulation model to investigate the ejection of hypervelocity stars from Andromeda's inner region, addressing the problem of identifying Andromeda as a potential origin for Milky Way HVSs with trajectories pointing toward the Galactic disc, which challenges the prevailing assumption that such stars primarily originate from the Milky Way's center. Data Availability: Data and code for the study are available on GitHub, with additional velocity data from Marchetti (2021) accessible on his website and Gaia DR3 data acquisition details provided in Appendix A. Result Discussion: The simulation finds that only about 0.013% to 0.011% of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) from Andromeda reach within 50 kpc of the Milky Way's center at present time, with fewer HVSs in the equal-mass scenario (2,346) compared to the half-mass scenario (1,891), due to stronger gravitational attraction in the equal-mass case and higher escape velocities in the half-mass scenario. Model and Simulations: The paper proposes a dynamical model that combines NFW and Plummer density profiles to compute the gravitational potential of the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies, enabling the simulation of hypervelocity star trajectories by solving the two-body problem with accurate mass distributions and accounting for the mutual gravitational influence of the galaxies. Appendix A Data Selection from Gaia DR3 Catalogue: This section describes the selection criteria used to extract high-precision astrometric and radial velocity data from the Gaia DR3 catalogue, filtering stars with reliable parallax, proper motion, and radial velocity measurements and a heliocentric velocity magnitude of at least 190 km/s to stay within the 3-million-object limit. Appendix B Coordinate Transformation of the Gaia DR3 Velocities: This section describes the coordinate transformation process to convert Gaia DR3 velocity data from equatorial to Galactic coordinates using parallax, proper motion, and radial velocity, involving multiple rotations and Cartesian velocity component calculations.
